role_summary: SNCC voter-registration demonstrator in Lowndes County, Alabama, at 17; survivor of the August 14, 1965 Hayneville incident when Jonathan Daniels was killed protecting her; founder (2007) of the SpiritHouse Project documenting state-sanctioned deaths of Black Americans; MDiv from Episcopal Divinity School (1998).
ai_reading: Sales sits at -1.00 on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis, the maximum reading toward Tactical Pragmatism in the entire 136-interview corpus. The cultural placement of Sales would predict the opposite: her post-movement career has been definitionally strategic, with the SpiritHouse Project's longitudinal documentation of state-sanctioned violence against Black Americans (over 2,000 cases catalogued since 2007) representing decades of long-arc strategic-vision work. The embedding reads her oral history as tactical-pragmatic instead, possibly catching the August 14, 1965 Hayneville scene that dominates her interview's most-cited passages, the single moment when Jonathan Daniels took the shot meant for her, as a tactical-immediate memory whose vector signature overwhelms the long-arc strategic framing of her later organizing. Her top semantic neighbors are Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 92), Evans Derrell Hopkins (entry 42), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7), Gwendolyn M. Patton (entry 52), and Harold K. Brown (entry 55), a broadly-SNCC cohort with some BPP-adjacent overlap (Hopkins), reading her as a movement voice whose cosine to the Mississippi voter-registration cohort is unexpectedly high given her Alabama anchor.
biographical_paragraph: Ruby Nell Sales was seventeen and on a voter-registration demonstration in Lowndes County, Alabama on August 14, 1965, when she and her group were released from a six-day jail stay without notice; at a nearby store, the state highway employee Tom Coleman threatened them with a shotgun, and fellow marcher Jonathan Daniels, a white Episcopal seminarian from Cambridge, Massachusetts, pushed her out of the way and took the shot meant for her [src: 2]. Daniels died instantly; Coleman was acquitted by an all-white Hayneville jury [src: 2]. Sales was born July 8, 1948 in Jemison, Alabama and attended Tuskegee Institute as her undergraduate institution; she later took degrees from Manhattanville College, Princeton University, and a Master of Divinity from Episcopal Divinity School in Cambridge in 1998 [src: 2]. In 2007 she founded the SpiritHouse Project, the nonprofit she has dedicated to Daniels's memory and which has documented more than two thousand state-sanctioned deaths of Black Americans, of which roughly 98 percent involved unarmed people [src: 3]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 112 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Ruby Sales oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Jonathan Daniels archival exhibit (the August 14, 1965 Hayneville incident is documented in the Episcopal Church's archival collection of Daniels's seminary correspondence and martyrdom record)
